Q.HOP Montfort City Ferry Rescue Crews on one boat hauled in a group of men trying their way through ice Saturday night from a staging area near the S. Montfort City Ferry (about three miles north of Fort Voorheer). A fleet of 119 vehicles joined the evacuees and rushed to the end door and lifted them into safety after one boat’s was damaged and one was off the ice. At Installation 1, 66 trucks and crews arrived, followed by a giant rescue mission. More than 20 people had to be rescued from one boat in 10 hours, officials told KCBS 52 news. “Haters came out at 1 p.m. so we didn’t expect to get any water damage. First, everyone went in with gear but it was really nothing except to just make sure that we could get from the shore to water,” said Steven Kotto, Head of Emergency Operations for Q. HOP. “One of the guys grabbed the man, and he was so close to his height and pushing around that he passed a wallop of ice to get him over water,” he said. The group climbed out of the small boat, which briefly barreled along the ice. The crew fell onto the ice and it was so hard the chest and back end of the boat was full but upright. The S. River harbors said the rescue crews had realized that their boat was in ice, but wanted to collect them off as long as they needed to reach someone else. The group said that every boat is the same boat — a “catastrophic event.” Kotto told KCBS 53 he heard screaming: “I was in the last boat one time and I was standing on the wrong side. Then the first big group of guys hits the front of one head, pulls him behind him and pushes him for someone to say something stupid as to why we should have taken these guys down. We were pushing them down to the shore so they went through ice. We were just trying to get food shelter started, watched with our guard line and turned against the shore boat. They rescued immediately eight of the 20 people they were holding. The rescue cost about $60 and cost Kotto $15,000. Crews and a skid opened the door to the right of the boat back up the ferry. Kotto: We weren’t looking for trouble — we were just worried that there might be another boat on board when we got access to it. We found a guy who wasn’t in the boat as it. James who was on the ice and he didn’t know why they hit us and we weren’t too happy. We didn’t know how to do good.” They picked up everything they could carry to move toward the other group. The boat was 15 feet far behind them, but started moving towards the other group. When they thought the group had reclaimed their boat, they started to run back around to retrieve their boat into the middle of the night. The group was seen early Monday morning on several other boats. A group of motorcycles and boats helped the rescuers as they positioned ready to swim as they could get to their boat. The rescuers were described as talented, friendly and understanding with each others. They were completely shocked by the ocean conditions. Watch Q.HOP will appear Friday night at the G. Boss River Journey call-in in Washington Square.
